summary: Gruber reviews the iPad Magic Keyboard, recounting how his initial disappointment with its stiff hinges and top-heaviness gave way to genuine enthusiasm within fifteen minutes. He methodically explains why the physics of attaching a heavy iPad to a lightweight keyboard base necessitate much stiffer hinges than a MacBook, and why this is ultimately the correct design choice. The review covers the dual-hinge mechanism, weight comparisons to MacBooks, the excellent keyboard and smaller trackpad, and the overall typing experience. He concludes that the Magic Keyboard transforms the iPad into a legitimate laptop replacement, calling the combination of hardware and iPadOS 13.4 trackpad support an altogether new and natural experience.
key_points: The Magic Keyboard's stiff hinges and strong magnets are physics-driven necessities, not flaws — Apple correctly erred on the side of too strong rather than too weak
The 12.9-inch iPad Pro with Magic Keyboard weighs about 8% more than a MacBook Air, making it a practical laptop replacement with no meaningful weight penalty
The Smart Keyboard is an iPad cover you can type on; the Magic Keyboard is a portable keyboard stand — fundamentally different products despite looking similar
The keyboard feel rivals or exceeds MacBook keyboards, while the trackpad is half the size of a MacBook Air's but functional with adjusted speed settings
Apple's 'Float' marketing ad sets misleading expectations about how effortlessly the display tilts, when in reality the stiffness is a deliberate and necessary feature
money_quotes: Going in with a set of expectations even loosely based on a hope like mine — for something that feels even vaguely MacBook-y — is like expecting a sip of piping hot coffee and it turns out your mug is filled with cold water. You instinctively reject it.
The iPad Magic Keyboard is to iPad-as-laptop accessories what AirPods were to earbuds: a game changer.
Apple erred on the side of making the hinges and magnets too strong, not too weak, and once you grok that it's a folding stand, not a folding cover, it is obvious that this is the correct design.
The Smart Keyboards are iPad covers you can type on. The Magic Keyboard is a portable keyboard stand, not a cover — when you want to use your iPad as a tablet, not a laptop, you must detach it.
I went back to the Smart Keyboard cover to type just this single paragraph. It's like comparing a meal at your favorite restaurant to a meal on an airplane. iPad Smart Keyboards are dead to me.

The combination of excellent hardware — truly exquisite, from the hinges and magnets to the keyboard and trackpad themselves — and outstanding pointer and gesture support in iPadOS 13.4 make it hard to believe we haven't been able to convert an iPad into a great laptop for years.
This is an altogether new experience with an iPad, but it's so natural it feels longstanding.
I wish the dark MacBooks were this dark instead of 'space gray', which is just slightly darker than regular aluminum. If you're going to go dark, go dark.
key_insight: The iPad Magic Keyboard's initially off-putting stiffness is actually brilliant engineering — once you understand it as a precision keyboard stand rather than a laptop-style cover, its physics-driven design choices reveal themselves as exactly right.
